dialogue.utterance: Hi, my name is Alex with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Her name is Monica. She's having trouble breathing and looked really out of sorts.
Is she experiencing any chest pains or dizziness?
She said it feels like she's in an asthma attack, but worse. She's been struggling for a few days now.
Monica, can you open your eyes for me?
My name's Monica. Ugh, I don't feel so great.
Can you tell me the date today and where we are?
November... 10th? I think we are on 9th and Tredegar in Richmond.
I see you're in a tripod position - leaning forward to help with your breathing. Is that right?
Yeah, this is the only way I can breathe without passing out.
Monica, how long has this been going on?
A few days... It got progressively worse. I ran out of my breathing treatments and tried using someone else's Albuterol, but it didn't help.
Have you experienced something like this before?
Yes, last year I was in the ICU. They had to intubate me. This feels the same.
Are you experiencing any pain or other symptoms besides the difficulty breathing?
No, just a tight feeling in my chest and really shallow breaths.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Any idea what caused it to flare up?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Not sure. I don't have a home, so I sleep outside. It might have been the cold or maybe an infection.
Your airway is clear. Are you able to talk in full sentences right now?
Yeah, it's rough, but I can say a few words.
Good. You're managing your own airway well. We'll continue to monitor it.
We're going to check your breath sounds. Can I listen for a moment?
Do what you need to.
Breathing sounds are present and equal in both lungs with wheezing on expiration.
Pulse is strong, but rhythm is irregular. Let me get my stethoscope.
Radial pulse is strong and irregularly irregular.
Your heart rate is 89 beats per minute. I'll check your blood pressure.
Blood pressure is 110 over 67. SpO2 is 99%.
You're breathing at 21 times a minute, with shallow, labored breaths.
I need a drop of blood to test your glucose. It's 0.
We'll hook you up to the EKG monitor. It shows a sinus arrhythmia with some irregular beats.
Do you use any medications regularly?
I use Albuterol and ProAir. But I've been out of them. I don't have much else.
Are you allergic to any medications?
No, I don't have any allergies.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
Maybe 12 hours ago. I wasn't hungry and just don't eat much.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - gross visual examination
dialogue.utterance: Are there any injuries to your head or neck?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - gross visual examination
dialogue.utterance: No, I haven't hit my head or neck.
Your breath sounds are clear with bilateral expiratory wheezing.
Let me get you on the 4-lead EKG. It shows sinus arrhythmia with occasional PVCs and PACs.
I'm going to start an IV in your left wrist. It's an extremity vein, normal and easily accessible.
I'll give you a nebulizer treatment with Duoneb to open up your airways.
Your breathing has increased to 34 times a minute. That's a significant change.
Blood pressure has gone up to 154 over 94. I'll monitor this closely.
EKG reflects a continued irregular rhythm, but no acute cardiac event noted.
Let me take a quick read on your oxygen saturation again. It's 100%. That's a good sign.
I'll administer 10 mg of Dexamethasone now via IV to help with the inflammation in your airways.
You said you felt nauseated. So, I'm going to give you 4 mg of Zofran intravenously. It's flushed with 5 ml of normal saline.
We'll be taking you to VCU ED. It's a good facility to handle a case like yours.
You're secured with straps and railings up just in case. Let's confirm all your vitals again before we head out.
You're alert and oriented. Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 - this is reassuring.
End-Tidal CO2 is 31 mmHg. Your pain level is 0, per the report.
Your blood sugar is stable and within normal range. I'm calling it in to VCU now.
Let me check your peripheral pulse. They are all good and symmetrical.
You mentioned you have a history of severe asthma. Is there anything else in your medical history I need to know about?
No, that's about it. I've had some back spasms from sleeping on the ground, but nothing serious.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam - gross visual examination; Medic
dialogue.utterance: No nasal, ear, or throat injuries observed.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam - foreign body; Medic
dialogue.utterance: Do you feel anything stuck in your nose or throat?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam - foreign body; Patient
dialogue.utterance: No, everything's clear.
Your skin is warm. No signs of cyanosis or unusual color.
No jaundice, pallor, or signs of skin rashes.
I don't notice skin redness, coldness, or fever.
I don't observe any masses in the neck area.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - pupil size
dialogue.utterance: Your pupils react well to light. They're equal and constricted.
Your heart sounds are clean, with occasional PACs or PVCs recorded.
Peripheral pulses are strong and even bilaterally.
No surgical sites noted.
I'm going to press gently on your abdomen. Any tenderness or discomfort?
No, just my breathing is the main issue.
The shape and size of your abdomen are normal. No bowels sounds heard now.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: If I move your fingers, do you feel that?

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Yeah, I do.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - peripheral pulses
dialogue.utterance: Peripheral pulses are strong and palpable on all limbs.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - wounds or abrasions
dialogue.utterance: No wounds, cuts, or sores observed on your arms or legs.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - vertebral column
dialogue.utterance: No signs of instability or damage found along the spine.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - vertebral column
dialogue.utterance: No pain there. A little tired from all the moving though.
Any issues with urination or frequency lately?
I've been trying to avoid the bathroom as much as possible.
Let me take another breath to get the latest CO2 reading. It's still at 31.
One more glucose check - it came back normal.
I need to adjust your straps a bit again as we make sure everything is secured for transport.
VCU ED is the closest appropriate facility for your condition. We won't be using the siren for this transport.
Let me update your EKG signal again. Still consistent with sinus arrhythmia and occasional PACs.
You don't appear dehydrated or have any acute skin changes.
All baseline assessments are complete. I'm finalizing documentation in the EPCR now.
I need your consent to transmit my final report over the phone to the receiving facility.
Just call it. I don't mind.
One final pulse check: 119 beats per minute.
I'll secure the straps once more and get ready to load you into the ambulance.
All vitals stable and interventions completed. We're all set for transport.
Your breathing remains at a higher rate, and we'll continue monitoring for any further changes in breathing pattern or effort.
Let me double-check your breath sounds. They're still equal with wheezing on expiration, consistent with asthma.
I just need a few minutes to wrap up documentation and make sure we're fully ready for the transfer.
Just rechecked your blood pressure. It remains at 154 over 94.
The hospitals have the patient status on file. Our transport to VCU is proceeding as planned.
All vital signs remain within normal limits given the condition and management.
We're heading directly to VCU ED for further care.
If anything feels different or worse during our ride, just tell me. I'll be monitoring you closely.
You're breathing better now. What's your current level of work with breathing?
It's improved a bit, but I'm still working hard to breathe.
Got it. We'll keep tracking. Every hour we'll reassess in full to be sure you're safe.
No new vital sign trends or concerns noted over the last hour.
You're doing well. Let's talk again in another hour to confirm everything's still smooth for you.
I just got a reply that VCU is accepting the patient and has everything prepared on our arrival.
The nurse at VCU has signed the handoff document. Good for our records.
We are at the hospital. Let's move smoothly into the transfer to the ER.
I'll call ahead one more time to confirm the patient report is being received as expected.
